title: skill-creator
subtitle: The skill that creates skills: you ask, you check, you improve

sources.title: Creating custom skills — Claude Help Center

theory.text: The meta-skill: a tool that makes tools

theory.md:
In the previous lesson you built a skill by hand — and that was important experience: now you understand every line of SKILL.md. But Anthropic's official library has a faster way: **skill-creator** — a skill whose job is to create other skills.

Inside skill-creator is packed the very expertise you spent three lessons learning: the format specification, the rules for name, the description formula, the principles of good instructions, the folder structure. When you ask Claude to "help me create a skill," skill-creator loads — and Claude turns into an experienced "skill engineer": it asks the right questions and then assembles a finished folder.

The result is a beautiful recursion: skills are such a universal format that even the ability to make skills is itself a skill.

theory.text: How to ask: give raw material, not a command

theory.md:
skill-creator doesn't need a perfect phrasing — it needs **raw material**: as many facts about your recurring task as possible. A good request contains:

- **The task:** what you do regularly ("every month I put together a report on the family's expenses").
- **The rules:** how the result should look ("spending categories, comparison with last month, three takeaways").
- **Example phrasings:** how you usually ask for this task — these are the future triggers of the description.
- **Materials, if any:** a template, an example of a finished result, a reference sheet.

Then a dialogue begins: skill-creator will clarify what's missing — edge cases, tone, what to avoid. Answer honestly and in detail: each of your answers turns into a line of instructions. This is an interview, not an exam — the more you tell it, the more precise the skill.

theory.title: A good request to skill-creator
theory.code:
Help me create a skill. Task: every week I write a
meal plan for a family of four.

Rules: 5 weeknight dinners, my daughter is allergic
to nuts, my husband doesn't eat fish, budget up to
$60, a shopping list as one block at the end.

I usually ask for it like this: "make a menu for the
week," "what to cook this week," "a dinner plan."

theory.md:
skill-creator will produce a finished folder with a SKILL.md. Don't accept it blindly — you already know enough to run a review. An acceptance checklist:

1. **The frontmatter is valid:** name in lowercase with hyphens, description in place, both `---` lines present.
2. **The description catches your phrasings:** compare it with the phrases you actually use to ask for the task. Your "triggers" from the request should be inside it.
3. **The instructions match reality:** read them like an editor. Did the generator add anything extra? Did it lose the nut allergy?
4. **No fluff:** paragraphs like "be helpful and friendly" can be safely deleted — they don't affect the result.

After the review comes the familiar loop from the previous lesson: connect it and run three tests (direct, indirect, off-topic request).

theory.text: How to improve it: the skill grows with you

theory.md:
The main advantage of the "you + skill-creator" combo shows up over time. Noticed the skill makes a mistake? Don't fix it by hand — describe the problem to Claude: "The menu skill keeps suggesting pasta, I'm tired of it. Add a variety rule: don't repeat the main dish more than once every two weeks." skill-creator will make the fix in the right place in the instructions.

New capabilities are added the same way: "Add seasonality support to the skill: more vegetables in summer, warming dishes in winter." The skill becomes a living document that accumulates your experience: each "not quite right" note makes all future results better.

And don't forget about other people's skills: skill-creator can not only create but also analyze. Downloaded a colleague's skill or a library example? Ask it to break it down: where the description is strong, what's missing in the instructions, what's worth adapting for you. It's a fast way to learn from finished work.

theory.bad: Make me some kind of useful skill about food.
theory.good: Help me create a skill for planning a family menu. Every week I ask for a plan of weeknight dinners: a family of four, my daughter is allergic to nuts, budget up to $60, and I need a shopping list at the end. I usually phrase it like this: "make a menu for the week," "what to cook this week."
theory.explanation: The first request forces skill-creator to invent both the task and the rules — you'll get a templated skill about nothing that you'll have to rewrite. The second gives raw material on all four points: task, rules, limits, real trigger phrasings. From such a request a skill is born that fires precisely and accounts for what matters specifically to your family.

rows: Full control, deep understanding of every line
A first learning skill; fine-tuning; simple skills
Fast; knows the spec and best practices; asks questions itself
Working skills; complex structure with resources; growing existing ones

theory.md: In the official anthropics/skills library, skill-creator is described as a skill for creating new skills and improving existing ones: it helps you go from an idea to a correct folder structure. That's exactly why it's useful to apply it to other people's skills too: ask it to analyze an existing SKILL.md — you'll get an analysis and improvement suggestions.

Even with a generator, the anatomy knowledge from lessons 3–4 hasn't gone stale: it turns you from a consumer into an editor. The difference is like between "something was generated for me" and "I accepted the work by a checklist." You always bear the final responsibility for the description and instructions — you're the one who has to live with this skill.

tasks.question: What is skill-creator?
options: An online builder on a separate website where skills are assembled with a mouse
An official skill from Anthropic's library that helps create and improve other skills
A paid API feature for auto-generating prompts
A built-in code editor in claude.ai
tasks.explanation: skill-creator is a meta-skill from the anthropics/skills library: an ordinary folder with a SKILL.md, inside which is the expertise for creating skills. When it loads, it turns Claude into a "skill engineer": an interview about the task, then a finished folder with a SKILL.md.
tasks.hint: Recall the "beautiful recursion" from the start of the lesson.

tasks.instruction: Assemble a strong request to skill-creator for a "job posts for our studio" skill. Order: task → rules → example phrasings.
blocks: Help me create a skill: I regularly publish job posts for our design studio.
Rules: a lively tone with no HR clichés, always a salary range, 3 "selling" facts about the studio, up to 800 characters.
I usually ask like this: "a job post," "we need a junior, write an announcement," "a hiring notice."
distractors: Make something cool for social media, you're smart.
Just don't ask me any questions, just hand over the finished file.
tasks.explanation: The request gives skill-creator all the raw material: the task, the result rules, and real phrasings — the future description triggers. The traps hurt in different ways: "something cool" deprives the generator of facts, and banning questions breaks skill-creator's main mechanism — the clarifying interview.

tasks.instruction: skill-creator generated the frontmatter for the skill from the previous task (job posts). You accept the work by the checklist. One line needs a fix — find it.
lines: description: Creates high-quality content to attract the best talent to the organization.
tasks.explanation: The line is formally correct, but in substance it's HR-cliché fluff: not a word about "job," "post," or "announcement," which you actually use to ask for the task. The request "we need a junior, write an announcement" won't match this description. The fix: "Writes job posts for a design studio. Use for requests: a job post, a hiring announcement, a notice about looking for an employee." Generators make mistakes too — that's why a review is mandatory.
tasks.hint: Check point 2 of the acceptance checklist: does the description catch your real phrasings?

tasks.instruction: The menu skill has been working for a week, and you've noticed a problem: every plan starts with pasta. Ask Claude to improve the skill — describe what's wrong and what rule to add.

steps.claudeReply:
Got the problem. Connecting **skill-creator** and making a fix in the menu skill — to the "Rules" section I'm adding:

```markdown
- Variety: don't repeat the main dish more often
  than once every 2 weeks; no more than one
  pasta dish in a single week
```

Notice what I did NOT touch: the description (the skill fires correctly — the problem was in the instructions, not the triggers) and the other rules (the nut allergy and budget stayed in place).

Check it on the next plan: if pasta shows up twice again — we'll come back and tighten the wording.
steps.failHint: Describe the problem and the desired rule, for example: "The menu skill has pasta every time. Add a rule: don't repeat the main dish more than once every two weeks."

tasks.successMessage: That's what mature work with skills looks like: noticed a problem → described it → the fix landed exactly in the right section. The skill got better for all future weeks, not for one response.

tasks.instruction:
Put skill-creator to the test on your own task. **A plan with skill support is required.**

1. **Pick a task** — it can be the same one as in the previous lesson (so you can compare the hand-made and generated results), or a new one.
2. **Write a raw-material request:** task + rules + your real phrasings (the future triggers) + materials, if any. Ask Claude to help create a skill.
3. **Go through the interview:** answer the clarifying questions in detail — each answer becomes a line of instructions.
4. **Accept the work by the checklist:** valid frontmatter, a description with your triggers, instructions with no inventions and no fluff.
5. **Demand at least one fix** — for example, ask it to remove an extra paragraph or add a missing rule. Check that the fix landed where it should.
checklist: The request had raw material: task, rules, and my real phrasings
I answered the clarifying questions instead of brushing them off
Checked the frontmatter: name is valid, description contains my trigger words
Read the instructions in full and found no invented "facts" about me
Requested at least one fix and confirmed it was applied precisely
